text: Hey everyone, today's video is about a laparoscopic left colectomy with a difficult splenic flexure mobilization.

This patient was diagnosed with synchronous colonic cancers on the left side. Unfortunately, the rectal cancer was not tattooed at the index colonoscopy which made the operation slightly more challenging.
If you like today's video, please subscribe to my channel by pressing the red button. Thank you.
Preoperative staging showed two cancers. One was in the rectum and the other one in the proximal sigmoid. Diagnostic laparoscopy showed that only the proximal sigmoid cancer was tattooed.
The surgeon begins the dissection by tenting up the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) and dividing it, using standard laparoscopic techniques.
The surgeon observes the mesentery and the trunk of the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA), noting the presence of abundant adipose tissue.
The surgeon navigates the dissection plane between the superior hypogastric plexus and the IMA pedicle, progressing towards the top of the total mesorectal excision (TME) plane.
The surgeon clears the adipose tissue from the trunk of the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA), revealing the vessel's structure.
The surgeon demonstrates the division of the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) into the left colic and sigmoid vessels, with the pedicle taken below the division near the aorta.
The surgeon lifts the pedicle of the divided inferior mesenteric vein and begins dissecting to locate the fascia of Toldt, separating the mesocolon from the underlying retroperitoneal structures.
The surgeon continues the dissection in a medial to lateral fashion towards the splenic flexure, aiming to mobilize it by following the established plane.
The surgeon employs a combination of blunt and sharp dissection techniques to separate tissue layers, using diathermy or an energy device when necessary to further develop the dissection plane.
A rigid sigmoidoscope is inserted intraoperatively to determine the position of the rectal cancer, which is located between the mid to upper rectum.

The surgeon diverts tension to the top of the total mesorectal excision (TME) plane, carefully navigating to preserve the hypogastric plexus and the right and left hypogastric nerves, which are visually demonstrated in the surgical field.
The surgeon demonstrates the correct dissection plane, which opens into the total mesorectal excision (TME) plane. The dissection is performed carefully to maintain a bloodless field.
After completing the posterior dissection, the surgeon opens the peritoneal reflection and continues dissection around the rectal tube to reach the appropriate surgical plane.
The surgeon demonstrates the right hypogastric nerve, which is clearly visible dividing into several small branches within the correct dissection plane.

The surgeon dissects up to the junction between the lower and mid rectum, opening the anterior plane. The mesorectum on both sides is prepared for division in anticipation of inserting the surgical stapler.
The surgeon begins dividing the left colon, carefully maintaining dissection within the plane of the peritoneal reflection to avoid entering an incorrect plane.
The surgeon opens a window into an incorrect plane during the dissection, but quickly recognizes and corrects the error to continue the procedure.

Had the dissection proceeded in the same plane, there is a danger of ending up behind the kidney. As described earlier, one of the advantages of doing a lot of medial to lateral dissection is that you can easily join the two planes with minimal left to right dissection as demonstrated in this case.
The surgeon performs a left to right dissection, successfully joining two planes, facilitating the approach towards the splenic flexure, which is visible in preparation for anticipated mobilization.
The surgeon continues dissection toward the splenic flexure, revealing that the splenic flexure is deeply set. The greater omentum is visibly adhered to the spleen.
The surgeon carefully turns inward from the left to right side, avoiding dissection toward the spleen, to preserve the integrity of the colon during the procedure.

The surgeon tilts the patient's head up and proceeds with dissection in the gastrocolic omentum, visibly opening the lesser sac. This step facilitates access to the spleen from the supracolic compartment, aiding further dissection.

The surgeon carefully dissects the greater omentum from the spleen, addressing dense adhesions that complicate mobilization from the supracolic compartment. The dissection proceeds from the infracolic region, moving from left to right to separate the omentum from the spleen.
please make sure you stay away from the spleen as any damage to the spleen or excessive traction on the greater omentum can cause hemorrhage.
The surgeon mobilizes the left colon, revealing the geratose fascia. Attention shifts to the rectum, where the remaining strands of mesorectum are carefully divided.

The surgeon demonstrates the left hypogastric nerve, carefully separating it from the mesorectal fascia. An endostapler is then used to divide the rectum, completing the division with multiple firings.

The surgeon demonstrates the mobilized colon reaching into the pelvic cavity without tension on the anastomosis. The anastomosis is submerged in water to check for air leaks, confirming its integrity.
